q: The magnifying power of an astronomical telescope is the ratio of the angle $\beta$ subtended by the image to the angle $\alpha$ subtended by the object.
A refracting astronomical telescope consists of an objective of short focal length.
The terrestrial telescope produces an inverted final image.
Binoculars use total reflecting prisms to reduce the length of the terrestrial telescope.
The Newtonian design of a reflecting telescope uses a parabolic objective mirror.
For an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment, the separation of the lenses is $L = f_o - f_e$.
Optical microscopes use photons or light energy.
The electron microscope can reach a magnification of 1,000,000.
Compound microscopes are primarily used to study the electron structure of metals and crystals.
In a compound microscope, the separation of the lenses is given by $L = v_o + u_e$.
The eyepiece of a refracting astronomical telescope has a long focal length.
Galileo invented the refracting telescope.
In a reflecting telescope, the concave mirror serves as the objective.
Binoculars consist of a pair of telescopes, one for each eye.
When the final image of an astronomical telescope is formed at infinity, the magnifying power is $MP = \frac{f_o}{f_e}$.
The magnifying power of a compound microscope in normal setting is $MP = \frac{v_o}{u_o} \times \frac{25}{f_e}$.
A terrestrial telescope uses a convex lens as an eyepiece to produce an erect image.
The electron beam in a transmission electron microscope carries information about the structure of the specimen.
For large magnifying power, the objective of a compound microscope should have a long focal length.
In a compound microscope, the magnifying power of the eyepiece is defined as $\frac{25}{u_e}$.
Electron microscopes can be used to investigate the ultrastructure of biological specimens.
The image produced by a compound microscope is real and inverted.
Reflecting telescopes are used at most astronomical observatories.
The objective of an astronomical telescope forms a real and inverted image.
In the Newtonian design, a 45 degree flat mirror is used to reflect light to the eyepiece.
Terrestrial telescopes are specifically designed to produce an erect image for viewing objects on Earth.
The magnifying power of a compound microscope is independent of the distance of the image from the eyepiece.
Electron microscopes use electromagnetic or electrostatic lenses.
The image formed by the objective lens of an astronomical telescope is at the focus of the objective.
